note_data.title: Gas exchange and smoking
keyPoints: All living organisms are made of cells that use ATP from respiration for energy-requiring processes
Eukaryotic cells contain a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, while prokaryotic cells do not contain membrane-bound organelles
Animal cells may contain cilia - small hair-like structures that enable movement of substances along the cell surface
Mitochondria have a double membrane with inner membrane folded into cristae, containing enzymes needed for respiration
Ribosomes are the site of protein synthesis, with prokaryotes having smaller 70S ribosomes and eukaryotes having larger 80S ribosomes
Viruses are non-living structures consisting of nucleic acid enclosed in a protein capsid, sometimes with a phospholipid envelope

definitions.definition: A more detailed structure of cells that can be obtained by using a microscope
Small hair-like structures composed of microtubules in a '9+2' formation that allow movement of substances along the surface of the cell
A protective protein coat that encloses the nucleic acid in viruses
Folded projections of the inner mitochondrial membrane

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of structure or process in logical sequence

modelAnswer.question: Explain how the structure of alveoli is adapted for efficient gas exchange. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Alveoli have thin walls (one cell thick) which provides a short diffusion distance for gases. They have a large surface area which allows more gas exchange to occur. Alveoli are surrounded by many capillaries which maintains steep concentration gradients.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Good blood supply/capillaries/maintains concentration gradients

commonMistakes: Confusing prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell features - remember prokaryotes have no membrane-bound organelles
Forgetting that viruses are non-living structures, not cells
Mixing up ribosome sizes - prokaryotes have 70S ribosomes, eukaryotes have 80S ribosomes in cytoplasm
examinerTips: Remember that both mitochondria and chloroplasts contain small circular DNA and 70S ribosomes
Focus on the structural differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells for comparison questions
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Diagram of alveolus showing: thin alveolar wall, capillary network, direction of oxygen and carbon dioxide diffusion, blood flow direction. Label: alveolar air space, epithelium, capillary, red blood cells.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how smoking affects gas exchange in the alveoli and describe three specific health consequences
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — a multiple choice question asking which component of cigarette smoke damages cilia
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q3(c) (4 marks) — students had to compare gas exchange efficiency between a healthy lung and a smoker's lung
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — candidates were asked to explain why smokers are more prone to respiratory infections and link this to structural damage

workedExample.question: Explain how smoking damages the gas exchange system and reduces its efficiency. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the key components damaged by smoking - alveoli, bronchioles, and cilia
Step 2: Explain specific damage to each structure with scientific detail
Step 3: Link structural damage to functional consequences for gas exchange
Step 4: Mention specific harmful substances in cigarette smoke
workedExample.answer: Smoking damages the gas exchange system in several ways. Tar deposits on alveolar walls, reducing their surface area and making them less permeable to gases. This decreases the efficiency of oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange. Toxic chemicals destroy the elastic fibers in alveoli, causing them to lose their shape and merge together, further reducing surface area. Smoking paralyzes and destroys cilia in the bronchi and bronchioles, preventing them from moving mucus containing trapped pathogens and particles upward. This leads to mucus accumulation and increases infection risk. Carbon monoxide in smoke combines irreversibly with hemoglobin, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity of blood. The reduced surface area, damaged air passages, and impaired oxygen transport all significantly decrease gas exchange efficiency.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: tar damage to alveoli, loss of surface area, destruction of elastic fibers, damage to cilia, mucus accumulation, carbon monoxide effects

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the effects of tar and carbon monoxide' — always specify that tar damages lung tissue while carbon monoxide affects oxygen transport in blood
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often gave vague answers about lung damage' — always mention specific structures like alveoli, cilia, and elastic fibers when explaining smoking effects

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently failed to link structural damage to functional consequences' — always explain how physical damage leads to reduced gas exchange efficiency, not just list the damage
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw and label a comparison diagram showing: (1) Healthy alveolus - thin walls, good blood supply, elastic fibers intact, large surface area; (2) Smoker's alveolus - thickened walls with tar deposits, damaged elastic fibers, reduced surface area, merged air sacs. Include bronchiole cross-sections showing healthy cilia vs damaged/paralyzed cilia. Add arrows showing efficient vs impaired gas exchange, and label key damaging substances (tar, carbon monoxide, nicotine).
note_data.quickRecap: All living organisms are made of cells that use ATP for energy. Eukaryotic cells have a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, while prokaryotic cells do not. Viruses are non-living structures with nucleic acid in a protein capsid. Microscopy enables examination of structures not visible to the naked eye.
